confidence_rationale: Annual Review of Psychology is a high-quality venue and Walker + Stickgold are senior figures in sleep + memory consolidation. The paper documents the cumulative-across-cycles pattern of sleep-dependent memory and plasticity that REE's F1 cross-cycle EMA architecture mirrors. Source quality high (Annu Rev Psychol; canonical review). Mapping fidelity moderate-high: the paper directly supports the architectural pattern of cumulative across-cycle accumulation of sleep effects, which is exactly the F1 EMA reference shape. Confidence below 0.85 because the paper is about memory consolidation broadly rather than specifically about precision recalibration; the architectural transfer is by analogy.
failure_signatures: If sleep-dependent memory consolidation turned out to be primarily WITHIN a single cycle (one REM episode produces the full effect, multiple cycles produce no additional effect), the cumulative-across-cycles reading that supports F1 would weaken; REE's _persistent_zero_point EMA across cycles would lose its biological motivation and a snapshot-only architecture might be more appropriate.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Sleep contributes importantly to processes of memory and brain plasticity. Sleep-dependent memory processing operates across multiple sleep cycles in a sustained, cumulative manner. Sleep-dependent perceptual and motor learning gains require multiple cycles of NREM and REM sleep; single-cycle interventions produce partial effects, while multi-cycle full-night sleep produces the full benefit. Sleep deprivation (selective REM or full-night) impairs the cumulative consolidation effect proportional to the deprivation magnitude.
mapping.ree_translation: The cumulative-across-cycles pattern of sleep-dependent memory + plasticity is the architectural shape REE's F1 EMA-tracked _persistent_zero_point implements. Each REM cycle's contribution to the persistent reference is a small fraction of the total (alpha=0.1 means each cycle contributes ~10% to the running EMA), and the effect ACCUMULATES across cycles -- which matches the Walker-Stickgold review's finding that sleep effects need multiple cycles to fully manifest. This is the dose-response signature that supports F1 over a snapshot-only architecture.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The paper is about memory consolidation (declarative + procedural) broadly, NOT specifically about precision-related recalibration. The architectural transfer to MECH-204's precision recalibration is by analogy: both share the cumulative-across-cycles dose-response pattern. The transfer assumes that the same cumulative signature applies to precision parameters as to memory traces; this is plausible (both are model parameters being refined by sleep) but not directly empirically established.
mapping.source_context: Annual Review of Psychology, comprehensive review of sleep + memory + plasticity literature through 2006. Walker + Stickgold are senior figures in this area. Human + animal evidence cited. No novel empirical data.

confidence_components.notes: Annu Rev Psych is a high-quality venue. Mapping fidelity moderate-high: the cumulative-across-cycles pattern is documented for memory consolidation; transfer to precision recalibration is by architectural analogy. Transfer risk low-moderate because the pattern (sleep cycles cumulatively refine model parameters) is broad enough to apply to both domains.
